clinicalStem: A 3-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department by his parents with a 12-hour history of high fever (39.8°C), worsening headache, and progressive neck stiffness. On examination he is irritable, photophobic, has a positive Kernig's sign, and a non-blanching petechial rash spreading across the trunk and lower limbs. His heart rate is 148 bpm, blood pressure 88/52 mmHg, and capillary refill time is 4 seconds. A lumbar puncture trolley is being prepared. What is the single most important immediate management step?
options: Urgent lumbar puncture to confirm the diagnosis before starting treatment
Intravenous ceftriaxone immediately, before lumbar puncture
Intravenous dexamethasone as the first drug to reduce inflammation
Blood cultures followed by immediate CT head before any antibiotics
Intravenous aciclovir pending viral encephalitis exclusion
explanation: In suspected bacterial meningitis with signs of septicaemia (non-blanching rash, haemodynamic compromise), intravenous antibiotics — ceftriaxone — must be given immediately without waiting for lumbar puncture or imaging. Delaying antibiotics by even 1 hour significantly worsens mortality and morbidity. Lumbar puncture can follow once the patient is stabilised, but it must never delay empirical treatment in this clinical picture.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Performing lumbar puncture first is dangerous in a haemodynamically unstable child with signs of septicaemia and raised ICP; antibiotic treatment must never be delayed for this procedure.
Correct — IV ceftriaxone must be given immediately; in bacterial meningitis every minute of antibiotic delay worsens outcome, and haemodynamic instability here makes LP deferral mandatory.

Dexamethasone is an adjunctive therapy that reduces inflammation and hearing loss in bacterial meningitis; however, it should be given alongside or just before the first antibiotic dose, not as the sole or first agent.

CT head is indicated when there are signs of raised ICP or focal neurology, but it must not delay antibiotics; blood cultures should be drawn but only if they do not cause a delay of more than 2–3 minutes.

IV aciclovir is important when herpes encephalitis is suspected (focal neurology, temporal lobe features), but this child's presentation (non-blanching rash, haemodynamic shock) strongly favours bacterial meningococcal septicaemia requiring ceftriaxone first.
clinicalPearl: In bacterial meningitis with septicaemia, IV ceftriaxone must be given immediately — never delay antibiotics for LP or CT when the child is haemodynamically unstable.
keyClue: Non-blanching petechial rash + haemodynamic compromise = meningococcal septicaemia requiring immediate IV ceftriaxone.
commonExamTrap: Waiting for lumbar puncture or CT scan before starting antibiotics — this is a potentially fatal delay in meningococcal disease.
missedConcept: Antibiotic-first principle: LP can follow after antibiotics once the patient is stable; treatment must never await diagnostic confirmation in a critically unwell child.
redFlagReason: Meningococcal septicaemia with non-blanching rash and haemodynamic instability; antibiotic delay is directly associated with death and severe disability.

clinicalStem: A 6-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever (38.6°C), personality change, and increasingly bizarre behaviour. Today she had a focal left-sided tonic-clonic seizure lasting 2 minutes. On examination she is confused (GCS 12/15), has left-sided facial droop and left arm weakness, and fundoscopy shows early papilloedema. An urgent MRI brain is ordered. Urgent CSF analysis shows lymphocytic pleocytosis (120 cells/mm³, predominantly lymphocytes), elevated protein (1.1 g/L), and normal glucose. CSF HSV PCR is pending. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Await HSV PCR result before starting any antiviral therapy
Start IV aciclovir empirically at 10 mg/kg every 8 hours
Start IV ceftriaxone and await CSF culture results
Administer IV methylprednisolone for autoimmune encephalitis
Start oral aciclovir and arrange outpatient neurology review
explanation: The clinical presentation — fever, personality change, focal seizure, focal neurological deficit, and CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein — is classic for HSV encephalitis. IV aciclovir at 10–15 mg/kg every 8 hours must be started empirically before HSV PCR results return, as the drug has a favourable safety profile and delay significantly increases mortality (from ~20% to ~70% untreated) and neurological sequelae. Treatment is continued or discontinued based on the PCR result.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Awaiting PCR results before starting aciclovir is dangerous; HSV encephalitis has high mortality and permanent neurological damage when treatment is delayed — empirical treatment is the standard of care.
Correct — IV aciclovir 10–15 mg/kg every 8 hours must be started empirically for suspected HSV encephalitis; the clinical and CSF picture strongly supports this diagnosis.

IV ceftriaxone covers bacterial meningitis, but this CSF profile (lymphocytes, high protein, normal glucose) with focal features and personality change is much more consistent with viral encephalitis, specifically HSV; bacterial meningitis would show neutrophilic pleocytosis and low glucose.

Autoimmune encephalitis (e.g., anti-NMDA receptor) is a consideration, but steroids must not be given before adequately treating HSV, as immunosuppression can worsen herpes encephalitis; steroids are a second step after antiviral cover.
Oral aciclovir does not achieve adequate CNS drug levels for encephalitis; IV administration is required, and the severity of this presentation mandates inpatient treatment.
clinicalPearl: Fever + personality change + focal seizure + CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis = empirical IV aciclovir immediately; never wait for PCR confirmation.
keyClue: Fever, personality change, focal neurological deficit, and lymphocytic CSF pleocytosis with elevated protein point to HSV encephalitis.
commonExamTrap: Withholding aciclovir until PCR returns — the risk of untreated HSV encephalitis far outweighs any risk from empirical aciclovir.
missedConcept: HSV encephalitis requires empirical IV aciclovir immediately; the high mortality of untreated disease mandates treatment before virological confirmation.
redFlagReason: Untreated HSV encephalitis carries ~70% mortality; delaying IV aciclovir pending PCR results is a life-threatening error.

clinicalStem: A 4-year-old boy from a country with high TB prevalence presents with a 3-week history of low-grade fever, headache, vomiting, and progressive drowsiness. He was incompletely vaccinated. On examination, GCS is 13/15, there is neck stiffness, and he has left CN VI palsy. CSF shows: lymphocytes 180 cells/mm³, protein 2.8 g/L, glucose 1.4 mmol/L (serum glucose 5.6 mmol/L). Acid-fast bacilli are seen on CSF smear. Anti-tuberculous therapy with HRZE is commenced. Which additional treatment is most appropriate to start alongside anti-TB therapy?
options: Intravenous aciclovir for 14 days
Intravenous ceftriaxone for 7 days
Oral prednisolone (or IV dexamethasone) for 4–6 weeks with gradual tapering
Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) for 2–5 days
Prophylactic phenytoin to prevent seizures
explanation: Adjunctive corticosteroids (dexamethasone or prednisolone) for 4–6 weeks are recommended in all stages of tuberculous meningitis. Evidence from randomised controlled trials shows that steroids significantly reduce mortality and severe neurological sequelae, likely by attenuating the inflammatory response that drives vasculitis, cerebral oedema, and raised ICP. They should be commenced alongside anti-TB therapy and then tapered over several weeks.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Aciclovir is the treatment for herpes encephalitis; it has no role in proven TB meningitis.
Ceftriaxone covers bacterial meningitis; once AFB have been identified and TB meningitis is confirmed, there is no indication for additional empirical bacterial cover.

Correct — adjunctive dexamethasone or prednisolone for 4–6 weeks with gradual taper is standard of care alongside anti-TB therapy in TB meningitis, reducing mortality and long-term neurological disability.
IVIG is used in immune-mediated conditions (e.g., Guillain-Barré, Kawasaki disease); it has no established role in tuberculous meningitis.
Prophylactic anticonvulsants are not routinely indicated in TB meningitis; seizures are managed if and when they occur, and the priority is anti-TB treatment plus corticosteroids.
clinicalPearl: Always add adjunctive corticosteroids (dexamethasone/prednisolone) to anti-TB therapy in TB meningitis — they reduce mortality and neurological disability regardless of disease stage.
keyClue: TB meningitis confirmed by AFB on CSF smear — the question tests knowledge of mandatory adjunctive steroid therapy.
commonExamTrap: Omitting steroids when anti-TB drugs are started, or substituting antibacterial cover instead of adding corticosteroids.
missedConcept: Corticosteroids are a class I recommendation in TB meningitis regardless of severity grade; they reduce the vasculitic and inflammatory injury that drives poor outcomes.

topic: Febrile seizure — acute management of prolonged febrile seizure
clinicalStem: A 2-year-old girl is brought by ambulance to the emergency department. Her parents report she had a generalised tonic-clonic seizure that has been ongoing for 12 minutes. Her temperature is 39.2°C. She has no prior seizure history, no focal neurological signs, and is otherwise a healthy child with normal development. She received no pre-hospital medication. She is still seizing on arrival. What is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Give rectal paracetamol to reduce the fever and observe whether the seizure stops
Perform urgent LP to exclude bacterial meningitis before giving any anticonvulsant
Administer IV or rectal diazepam (or buccal/intranasal midazolam) immediately
Intubate and ventilate immediately as this is status epilepticus
Give oral ibuprofen and place in recovery position; febrile seizures are always self-limiting
explanation: A febrile seizure lasting ≥5 minutes is classified as a prolonged febrile seizure (febrile status epilepticus) and requires immediate anticonvulsant treatment. First-line therapy is IV diazepam (0.3 mg/kg) or, when IV access is unavailable, rectal diazepam or buccal/intranasal midazolam. Antipyretics do not terminate seizure activity, LP must not precede seizure control in an actively seizing child, and intubation is a last resort reserved for refractory status.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Paracetamol reduces fever but does not terminate a seizure; it is not an anticonvulsant and would not stop ongoing status febrilis.
LP is contraindicated in an actively seizing child; seizure control is the immediate priority, and LP can be considered after stabilisation if bacterial meningitis is suspected.
Correct — IV diazepam or buccal/intranasal midazolam is the first-line emergency treatment for a prolonged febrile seizure (≥5 minutes); immediate intervention is required.
Intubation is reserved for refractory status epilepticus failing first- and second-line anticonvulsants; benzodiazepines should be tried first.
Febrile seizures are not always self-limiting — those lasting ≥5 minutes require active anticonvulsant treatment; prolonged seizures cause neuronal injury and must be terminated.
clinicalPearl: A febrile seizure lasting ≥5 minutes is an emergency — give IV diazepam (or buccal midazolam if no IV access) immediately; do not wait for it to stop spontaneously.
keyClue: Seizure still ongoing at 12 minutes in a febrile toddler — this is prolonged febrile status requiring immediate benzodiazepine.
commonExamTrap: Using antipyretics as first-line treatment for an active seizure — antipyretics treat fever but do not abort seizure activity.
missedConcept: Prolonged febrile seizures (≥5 minutes) require immediate anticonvulsant therapy; the threshold for treatment is lower than for afebrile status epilepticus.
redFlagReason: Febrile status epilepticus causes neuronal injury if not terminated; benzodiazepine must be given without delay.

topic: Simple febrile seizure — post-ictal management and parental counselling
clinicalStem: An 18-month-old boy is brought to the emergency department after a 90-second generalised tonic-clonic seizure that stopped spontaneously. His temperature was 38.7°C at onset. He is now post-ictal but responsive; he has no neck stiffness, no focal neurology, and no rash. He is fully vaccinated and was well until today. His development is normal. The seizure is his first. After confirming recovery, what is the MOST appropriate next step in management?
options: Admit for 48-hour EEG monitoring to characterise the seizure type
Perform lumbar puncture routinely to exclude occult meningitis in all children under 18 months
Investigate and treat the underlying source of fever; reassure and educate the parents
Start prophylactic sodium valproate to prevent recurrence
Arrange urgent MRI brain to exclude a structural cause
explanation: This is a simple febrile seizure — generalised, brief (<15 minutes), single episode within 24 hours, with full recovery and no meningeal signs. The appropriate management is to identify and treat the source of fever (e.g., viral URTI, UTI), advise parents on first aid and recurrence risk (~30–35%), and reassure them that simple febrile seizures do not cause brain damage or epilepsy in most children. Routine LP, EEG, neuroimaging, and prophylactic anticonvulsants are not indicated.
wrongAnswerExplanations: EEG is not indicated in a simple febrile seizure; it does not predict recurrence or future epilepsy and is not recommended by guidelines.

LP is not routinely indicated in a well-appearing, fully immunised child ≥18 months after a simple febrile seizure with no meningeal signs; guidelines recommend LP only if there are clinical features of meningitis.
Correct — identify and treat the fever source, provide parental education about first aid, recurrence risk (~30–35%), and reassurance that simple febrile seizures have an excellent prognosis.
Long-term prophylactic anticonvulsants (valproate, phenobarbitone) are not recommended for simple febrile seizures; the risk of drug side effects outweighs the modest reduction in recurrence.
Routine neuroimaging is not indicated after a simple febrile seizure with complete recovery and no focal neurological signs.
clinicalPearl: Simple febrile seizure (generalised, brief, single, full recovery) requires no investigations beyond treating the fever source; educate parents and reassure — no anticonvulsants, no imaging, no LP if meningism is absent.
keyClue: First generalised seizure <2 minutes, fully vaccinated, post-ictal recovery with no meningeal signs = simple febrile seizure requiring parental education, not investigation.
commonExamTrap: Recommending routine LP in a well-appearing fully immunised child over 18 months with simple febrile seizure and no signs of meningitis.
missedConcept: The distinction between simple and complex febrile seizures determines management; simple febrile seizures need no anticonvulsants, no imaging, and no LP unless clinical red flags are present.

topic: Childhood absence epilepsy — first-line drug treatment
clinicalStem: A 7-year-old girl is referred to the paediatric neurology clinic by her teacher, who noticed she frequently 'blanks out' for 5–10 seconds during class, sometimes up to 20 times per day, with no warning and immediate full recovery. Her mother has noticed her staring episodes at home. EEG shows generalised 3 Hz spike-and-wave discharges during hyperventilation. MRI brain is normal. Development and neurological examination are normal. What is the most appropriate first-line antiepileptic drug?
options: Carbamazepine
Sodium valproate
Ethosuximide
Levetiracetam
Phenytoin
explanation: Childhood absence epilepsy (CAE) is characterised by brief, frequent, generalised absence seizures with 3 Hz spike-and-wave on EEG. Ethosuximide is the drug of first choice when absence seizures are the only seizure type, as it is highly effective and has a more favourable side-effect profile than valproate in this age group. Sodium valproate is an alternative (or first choice if tonic-clonic seizures co-exist). Carbamazepine and phenytoin are contraindicated as they can worsen absence seizures.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Carbamazepine is used for focal epilepsies but is contraindicated in absence epilepsy — it can exacerbate absence seizures and is not a correct choice here.

Sodium valproate is an effective alternative for CAE, especially when generalised tonic-clonic seizures co-exist; however, ethosuximide is preferred for pure absence epilepsy due to its superior safety profile, particularly hepatotoxicity and teratogenicity concerns with valproate.

Correct — ethosuximide is first-line for childhood absence epilepsy with pure absence seizures; it selectively suppresses T-type calcium channels in thalamic neurons and has an excellent efficacy and safety profile.
Levetiracetam has some evidence in juvenile myoclonic epilepsy and can be used off-label in absence epilepsy, but it is not first-line for CAE.
Phenytoin is effective for tonic-clonic and focal seizures but does not treat absence seizures and may worsen them.
clinicalPearl: Ethosuximide is first-line for pure childhood absence epilepsy; avoid carbamazepine and phenytoin as they worsen absence seizures.
keyClue: Frequent brief staring episodes + 3 Hz spike-and-wave on EEG = childhood absence epilepsy requiring ethosuximide.
commonExamTrap: Choosing carbamazepine (used for focal epilepsy) for absence epilepsy — it worsens absence seizures.
missedConcept: Carbamazepine and phenytoin are contraindicated in absence epilepsy; ethosuximide (or valproate if generalised seizures also present) is the correct choice.

topic: Lennox-Gastaut syndrome — management of drop attacks
clinicalStem: A 5-year-old boy with intellectual disability has been having multiple daily seizure types including tonic, atonic (drop attacks), and atypical absence seizures since the age of 3. His EEG shows slow spike-and-wave (<2.5 Hz) and generalised polyspike discharges during sleep. MRI brain shows diffuse cortical atrophy. He has failed trials of valproate and clobazam. His parents are particularly concerned about the frequent drop attacks causing head injuries. Which antiepileptic drug has the strongest evidence for reducing drop attacks specifically in this syndrome?
options: Carbamazepine
Ethosuximide
Rufinamide
Phenytoin
Topiramate alone
explanation: Lennox-Gastaut syndrome (LGS) is a severe childhood epileptic encephalopathy characterised by multiple seizure types (tonic, atonic, atypical absence), slow spike-and-wave EEG, and intellectual disability. Rufinamide is specifically licensed as adjunctive therapy for LGS and has the strongest evidence base for reducing the frequency of tonic and atonic (drop) seizures in this syndrome. Valproate and clobazam remain first-line, but after their failure, rufinamide is the preferred add-on.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Carbamazepine is used for focal and tonic-clonic seizures but can paradoxically worsen generalised seizures (tonic, atonic) in LGS and is not recommended.
Ethosuximide targets absence seizures (3 Hz spike-wave) but is not effective for tonic or atonic seizures in LGS, where slow spike-wave predominates.

Correct — rufinamide is specifically licensed as adjunctive treatment for Lennox-Gastaut syndrome and has the strongest evidence for reducing drop attacks (tonic and atonic seizures) in this refractory epileptic encephalopathy.
Phenytoin can worsen atonic and myoclonic seizures and has no established benefit in LGS; it is not recommended.
Topiramate has evidence as adjunctive therapy in LGS, but rufinamide has superior and more specific evidence for reducing drop attacks in this syndrome following failure of first-line agents.
clinicalPearl: Rufinamide is the preferred adjunctive agent specifically licensed for Lennox-Gastaut syndrome, with the strongest evidence for reducing tonic and atonic (drop) seizures.
keyClue: Multiple seizure types + slow spike-and-wave + intellectual disability + failed valproate and clobazam = Lennox-Gastaut syndrome requiring rufinamide as adjunctive therapy.
commonExamTrap: Choosing carbamazepine or phenytoin for LGS — both can paradoxically worsen generalised seizures in this syndrome.
missedConcept: LGS is a specific epileptic encephalopathy with a targeted treatment algorithm; rufinamide, lamotrigine, and topiramate have adjunctive roles, with rufinamide having the most specific evidence for drop attacks.

clinicalStem: A 7-month-old boy is brought to the paediatrician because his parents have noticed brief clusters of sudden flexion of the trunk and extension of the arms ('jack-knife' movements) occurring 5–10 times per episode, with several clusters per day, over the past 3 weeks. His developmental milestones were normal until 2 months ago when his parents noted developmental regression. EEG shows hypsarrhythmia. MRI brain is normal (cryptogenic). What is the most appropriate treatment?
options: High-dose oral prednisolone or intramuscular ACTH
Oral sodium valproate as monotherapy
Oral ethosuximide
Phenobarbitone monotherapy
Watchful waiting for 4 weeks before starting treatment
explanation: Infantile spasms (West syndrome) is characterised by the triad of epileptic spasms, hypsarrhythmia on EEG, and developmental regression. Prompt treatment is essential because delay worsens neurodevelopmental outcomes. First-line treatment is high-dose oral prednisolone or intramuscular ACTH, both of which have strong evidence for spasm cessation and EEG resolution. Vigabatrin is an alternative (and first-line for tuberous sclerosis-associated spasms). Early and effective treatment is associated with better developmental outcomes.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Correct — high-dose prednisolone (oral) or ACTH (IM) is first-line for infantile spasms; rapid initiation is critical as treatment delay worsens neurodevelopmental prognosis.
Sodium valproate is not a first-line agent for infantile spasms; it may have some activity but lacks the high-level evidence of hormonal therapy or vigabatrin in this syndrome.
Ethosuximide is used for absence epilepsy with 3 Hz spike-wave and has no role in infantile spasms with hypsarrhythmia.
Phenobarbitone is used for neonatal seizures and some focal epilepsies; it is not effective as monotherapy for West syndrome.

Watchful waiting is never appropriate in West syndrome; delayed treatment directly correlates with worse cognitive and developmental outcomes, and treatment must be started as soon as the diagnosis is confirmed.
clinicalPearl: West syndrome (infantile spasms + hypsarrhythmia + developmental regression) requires immediate treatment with high-dose prednisolone or ACTH — delay worsens neurodevelopmental outcome.
keyClue: Jack-knife spasms + hypsarrhythmia on EEG + developmental regression in an infant = West syndrome requiring urgent hormonal therapy.
commonExamTrap: Treating infantile spasms with standard anticonvulsants (valproate, ethosuximide) rather than hormonal therapy (ACTH/prednisolone) or vigabatrin.
missedConcept: West syndrome is a time-sensitive epileptic encephalopathy; ACTH/prednisolone or vigabatrin must be started immediately — outcome is directly tied to rapidity of spasm cessation.

topic: Early-onset neonatal sepsis — empirical antibiotic regimen
clinicalStem: A male neonate is born at 38 weeks gestation via spontaneous vaginal delivery. His mother had a temperature of 38.5°C during labour, prolonged rupture of membranes for 22 hours, and was GBS-positive on antenatal swab but did not receive intrapartum prophylaxis. At 4 hours of life the baby develops grunting respirations, tachycardia (HR 190 bpm), temperature of 38.2°C, poor feeding, and capillary refill of 4 seconds. Blood culture is taken. What is the most appropriate immediate empirical antibiotic regimen?
options: Oral amoxicillin alone and monitor in the neonatal unit
Intravenous benzylpenicillin (penicillin G) and gentamicin
Intravenous ceftriaxone alone
Intravenous vancomycin and meropenem
Intramuscular ampicillin and await blood culture sensitivity
explanation: Early-onset neonatal sepsis (within 72 hours of birth) is most commonly caused by Group B Streptococcus (GBS) and Gram-negative enteric organisms, particularly E. coli. The recommended empirical regimen is intravenous benzylpenicillin (IV penicillin G) plus an aminoglycoside (gentamicin), which provides synergistic coverage against GBS (penicillin-sensitive) and Gram-negative organisms. Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates due to risk of bilirubin displacement from albumin. Oral antibiotics are inappropriate in a septic neonate.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Oral antibiotics have inadequate bioavailability and are completely inappropriate in a haemodynamically compromised septic neonate; IV treatment is mandatory.

Correct — IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin is the standard empirical regimen for early-onset neonatal sepsis; it covers GBS (the most common causative organism in this setting) and Gram-negative enteric pathogens synergistically.

Ceftriaxone is avoided in neonates because it displaces bilirubin from albumin-binding sites, increasing the risk of kernicterus; it is not recommended as empirical monotherapy in the neonatal period.
Vancomycin and meropenem are reserved for late-onset hospital-acquired sepsis or Gram-negative bacteraemia with resistance patterns; they are not first-line for early-onset neonatal sepsis.

Ampicillin can be used instead of benzylpenicillin as an alternative (ampicillin + gentamicin is also an acceptable regimen in some guidelines), but intramuscular administration is suboptimal in a septic neonate — IV is required; waiting for sensitivities before starting antibiotics in a septic baby is dangerous.
clinicalPearl: Early-onset neonatal sepsis: start IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin immediately after blood cultures; never use ceftriaxone in neonates due to bilirubin displacement risk.
keyClue: GBS-positive mother, prolonged ROM, no intrapartum prophylaxis, and sick neonate at 4 hours = early-onset GBS sepsis requiring IV benzylpenicillin + gentamicin.
commonExamTrap: Using ceftriaxone in a neonate — it is contraindicated due to bilirubin displacement and risk of kernicterus.
missedConcept: Ceftriaxone is contraindicated in neonates; the empirical regimen for early-onset neonatal sepsis is IV benzylpenicillin (or ampicillin) plus gentamicin.
redFlagReason: Neonatal sepsis is rapidly fatal; any delay in IV antibiotics or use of an inappropriate regimen (e.g., ceftriaxone causing kernicterus) is life-threatening.

clinicalStem: A 3-week-old girl born at term is referred for ophthalmological review after a neonatologist noticed bilateral chorioretinitis on fundoscopy during a routine check. The neonate has mild hepatosplenomegaly. CT head shows periventricular calcifications. Her mother had a cat and ate undercooked meat during the first trimester. Maternal and neonatal Toxoplasma IgM and IgG serology is positive. The baby is otherwise feeding well and gaining weight. What is the correct treatment?
options: No treatment required if the baby is asymptomatic apart from ocular findings
Pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid for 12 months
Pyrimethamine alone for 6 weeks
Spiramycin monotherapy for 12 months
High-dose intravenous cotrimoxazole for 6 weeks
explanation: Congenital toxoplasmosis requires treatment with the combination of pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid (leucovorin) for 12 months. This regimen significantly reduces the risk of progressive chorioretinitis and neurodevelopmental complications. Folinic acid (not folic acid) is added to counteract the antifolate effect of pyrimethamine on the neonate's bone marrow. Even mildly symptomatic infants should be treated for the full 12 months.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Even asymptomatic congenital toxoplasmosis should be treated; chorioretinitis and neurological calcifications are markers of active infection that will progress without treatment.
Correct — pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid for 12 months is the standard of care for congenital toxoplasmosis; this regimen reduces chorioretinal and neurodevelopmental progression.
Pyrimethamine monotherapy is insufficient; it must always be paired with sulfadiazine and folinic acid, and the duration should be 12 months, not 6 weeks.

Spiramycin is used in pregnant women to reduce maternal-to-fetal transmission, but it does not adequately treat established congenital infection in the neonate; pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid is required.
High-dose cotrimoxazole is not the standard regimen for congenital toxoplasmosis; it may have some activity against Toxoplasma but does not replace the established triple-drug protocol.
clinicalPearl: Congenital toxoplasmosis (chorioretinitis + periventricular calcifications + positive serology) requires 12 months of pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid, even when mildly symptomatic.
keyClue: Bilateral chorioretinitis + periventricular calcifications + positive Toxoplasma serology in a neonate = congenital toxoplasmosis requiring 12-month triple therapy.
commonExamTrap: Confusing spiramycin (used in pregnancy to prevent fetal infection) with the neonatal treatment regimen (pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid).
missedConcept: Folinic acid (leucovorin) must accompany pyrimethamine to protect against bone marrow suppression; folic acid is not a substitute in this context.

topic: Neonatal herpes simplex — recognition and treatment
clinicalStem: A 10-day-old male neonate, born by normal vaginal delivery to a mother with no known history of herpes, presents with irritability, poor feeding, and temperature instability (temperature 38.8°C). On examination he has a vesicular rash on the scalp and periorbital area, hepatomegaly, and appears jaundiced. Bloods show elevated liver enzymes (ALT 420 U/L, AST 380 U/L), thrombocytopenia (platelets 68 × 10⁹/L), and a coagulopathy. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis. HSV PCR from the vesicle swab is pending. What is the most appropriate immediate treatment?
options: Start IV ceftriaxone while awaiting CSF culture
Start IV aciclovir at 20 mg/kg every 8 hours immediately
Await HSV PCR results before starting any antiviral therapy
Administer IV immunoglobulin as the first-line treatment
Start oral aciclovir 200 mg 5 times daily
explanation: Neonatal herpes simplex infection (HSV) can present as disseminated disease with hepatitis, coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, vesicular rash, and CNS involvement. Untreated disseminated neonatal HSV has a mortality exceeding 80%. IV aciclovir at 20 mg/kg every 8 hours (higher dose than adult dosing) must be started immediately when neonatal HSV is suspected — before PCR results return. Duration is typically 21 days for CNS/disseminated disease and 14 days for skin-eye-mouth disease.
wrongAnswerExplanations: IV ceftriaxone is inappropriate here and is contraindicated in neonates due to bilirubin displacement risk; the clinical picture (vesicular rash, hepatitis, coagulopathy, lymphocytic CSF) strongly suggests disseminated neonatal HSV, not bacterial sepsis.

Correct — IV aciclovir 20 mg/kg every 8 hours must be started immediately for suspected disseminated neonatal HSV; the very high mortality of untreated disease mandates empirical treatment before PCR confirmation.
Awaiting PCR results is dangerous; neonatal HSV with disseminated disease and CNS involvement has >80% mortality untreated — empirical IV aciclovir must be started without delay.
IV immunoglobulin is not the first-line or standard treatment for neonatal HSV; it has no established role in this context.
Oral aciclovir does not achieve adequate plasma or CNS concentrations for neonatal HSV; IV administration is mandatory for all forms of neonatal herpes infection.
clinicalPearl: Neonatal HSV with disseminated disease requires immediate IV aciclovir 20 mg/kg every 8 hours — the dose is higher than in older children, and treatment must start before PCR confirmation.
keyClue: Vesicular rash + hepatitis + coagulopathy + thrombocytopenia + lymphocytic CSF in a neonate = disseminated HSV requiring immediate IV aciclovir.
commonExamTrap: Awaiting PCR results before starting aciclovir — disseminated neonatal HSV is rapidly fatal without treatment.
missedConcept: The dosing of IV aciclovir for neonatal HSV is 20 mg/kg every 8 hours — higher than standard adult or older-child dosing — and is given for 21 days for CNS or disseminated disease.
redFlagReason: Disseminated neonatal HSV has >80% mortality if untreated; delay in IV aciclovir is directly life-threatening.

subtopic: Sodium channel blockers worsening Dravet syndrome
clinicalStem: A 14-month-old girl is brought to the paediatric neurology clinic. She had her first seizure at 6 months of age — a prolonged hemi-clonic convulsion during a febrile illness. She has since had multiple prolonged febrile and afebrile seizures of various types (myoclonic, absence, focal, and generalised). Genetic testing reveals a de novo SCN1A pathogenic variant. Her mother asks whether carbamazepine, which helped a family member with epilepsy, could be used. Which of the following best describes the role of carbamazepine in this child's epilepsy?
options: Carbamazepine is the preferred first-line drug for Dravet syndrome
Carbamazepine can be used safely as adjunctive therapy once valproate fails
Carbamazepine is contraindicated in Dravet syndrome and may worsen seizure frequency
Carbamazepine is safe but offers no advantage over valproate
Carbamazepine should be used only for focal seizures but not for myoclonic components
explanation: Dravet syndrome is caused by loss-of-function mutations in SCN1A (sodium channel Nav1.1), predominantly expressed in inhibitory interneurons. Sodium channel blockers such as carbamazepine, lamotrigine, and phenytoin paradoxically worsen seizures in Dravet syndrome by further reducing sodium channel activity in inhibitory neurons, thereby increasing cortical excitability. These drugs are contraindicated. First-line treatments include valproate, clobazam, and stiripentol.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Carbamazepine is not first-line for Dravet syndrome; it is in fact contraindicated due to paradoxical seizure worsening via sodium channel blockade in inhibitory interneurons.
Carbamazepine must be avoided in Dravet syndrome even as adjunctive therapy; there is no safe role for sodium channel blockers (carbamazepine, lamotrigine, phenytoin) in this condition.

Correct — carbamazepine is contraindicated in Dravet syndrome (SCN1A loss-of-function); it blocks Nav1.1 channels in GABAergic inhibitory interneurons, paradoxically increasing seizure frequency and severity.
Carbamazepine is neither safe nor appropriate in Dravet syndrome; it can precipitate status epilepticus and is listed among the drugs to avoid in this genetically defined epilepsy.
There is no safe partial indication for carbamazepine in any seizure type within Dravet syndrome; the SCN1A mechanism means sodium channel blockade globally worsens the epilepsy.
clinicalPearl: In Dravet syndrome (SCN1A mutation), sodium channel blockers — carbamazepine, lamotrigine, phenytoin — are contraindicated and worsen seizures by blocking inhibitory interneuron sodium channels.
keyClue: SCN1A de novo pathogenic variant with multiple seizure types from infancy = Dravet syndrome; sodium channel blockers are contraindicated.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing carbamazepine for focal seizures in Dravet syndrome because it works for focal epilepsy in general — in SCN1A disease, it is dangerous.
missedConcept: Dravet syndrome (SCN1A) is an inhibitory neuron channelopathy; blocking sodium channels further impairs inhibition, paradoxically worsening seizures — treat with valproate, clobazam, and stiripentol instead.
